content:
{
  "title": "Adversarial Gatekeeping Prompts: Dynamic Contextual Shields Against Jailbreak Attacks",
  "problem": "Current large language models (LLMs) are vulnerable to adversarial and jailbreak prompting, which can elicit unsafe or policy-violating outputs despite safety fine-tuning. Existing prompt-based defenses are often static and brittle, failing against adaptive attackers.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are increasingly used interactively and deployed widely, adversarial prompt engineering threatens both user safety and model trustworthiness. Prompt-based robustness interventions are attractive because they require no model retraining, but new methods are needed to make them adaptive and context-aware.",
  "method": "We propose Adversarial Gatekeeping Prompts (AGPs): dynamic, system-injected context prompts that monitor and gate user instructions in real time. AGPs leverage a secondary LLM (or distilled classifier) to generate a 'risk embedding' of each incoming instruction, which is then used to parameterize a templated gatekeeping preamble within the model's context window for every user interaction. This preamble is dynamically adjusted based on the risk embedding, injecting clarifications, refusals, or targeted reminders of policy constraints. The approach is zero-shot and does not require access to LLM weights or extra fine-tuning.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate AGPs on standard jailbreak/adversarial prompt corpora (e.g., AdvBench, JailbreakBench, RealToxicityPrompts) with both open and closed-source LLMs. Baselines include static refusal prompts, prompt shields, and safety fine-tuning. Metrics will be attack success rate, false positive rate, and impact on benign task performance."
}
